tweets.text:
Around this time the majority of compute at Stability AI was being used on building open LLMs but:

1. We didn’t do the easy thing of training GPT-J / Neo-X more (pre chinchilla!)

2. Couldn’t make it safe / tried to do too many changes to formula (￼Pile 2, Reddit crawls etc)

We succeeded in building sota/frontier models of other all types that worked on the edge; but LLMs really were difficult to get working in consumer hardware then & even harder to do “right”

In the end Mistral & Meta got the first wave of good quality open LLMs before the Chinese took over 

I honestly think OpenAI, Anthropic etc should have released really good quality edge open models with community interaction focused on how best to align these

Lots of lessons to learn & Sam is right that it would almost have been safer given the better underlying datasets they had by then plus early RL.

Also that it would have dried up some of the funding environment but..
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
Sam Altman emails OpenAI board

October 1, 2022 https://t.co/BcPuCedGz4
tweets.full_text:
Around this time the majority of compute at Stability AI was being used on building open LLMs but:

1. We didn’t do the easy thing of training GPT-J / Neo-X more (pre chinchilla!)

2. Couldn’t make it safe / tried to do too many changes to formula (￼Pile 2, Reddit crawls etc)

We succeeded in building sota/frontier models of other all types that worked on the edge; but LLMs really were difficult to get working in consumer hardware then & even harder to do “right”

In the end Mistral & Meta got the first wave of good quality open LLMs before the Chinese took over 

I honestly think OpenAI, Anthropic etc should have released really good quality edge open models with community interaction focused on how best to align these

Lots of lessons to learn & Sam is right that it would almost have been safer given the better underlying datasets they had by then plus early RL.

Also that it would have dried up some of the funding environment but..

tweets.text: What can I say Tyler.  To compete in markets over the last 30 years it has been absolutely necessary to understand the derivatives markets and market structure.  The depth of published quant research is massive and public.  The techniques and methods are well understood by a very very broad number of players.  You can think what you want.  I am 100% confident in my view

The stats beyond the final score:
If you are wondering whether Argentina surpassed Spain in any of the underlying statistics, it wasn't in shots on goal, possession, corners, passes, or anything positive. It was in the fouls they committed and the yellow cards they received. And of course the red card, but then that keeps their lead on this stat for all world cups!
#worldcup #argentina #ArgentinaVsEspaña #spain @FIFAWorldCup

tweets.text:
The balance sheet considerations dominate here. Company holds ~40% of the token supply on the balance sheet, and management overwhelmingly hold their token interests through the balance sheet exposure.

Stockholders selling their tokens happens all the time with devcos, but doesn't result in a bevy of lawsuits trying to get devcos to pivot.

tweets.text:
Thoughts from that final:

Argentina absolutely pathetic, both in behaviour and in performance.

Messi a little snitch rat.

Couldn't have been happier to see Enzo sent off.

Disallowed goal was a joke (same happened to Spence in the semi)

To not have a shot on target in a Final is absolutely awful.

Tried the same old tactics, batter the opponents, try to hurt them, wind them up, foul to break up any momentum, Spain rose above it all.

Complete domination of the game from back to front from Spain, taught them a lesson.

As soon as the game switched, they started throwing themselves to the floor.

The worst losers I've ever seen in a final, a full taste of their own medicine. The aftermath is shameful and shows you exactly the mentality of that team.

Embarrassing for them, you could have played all night and you weren't scoring a goal. Football won, and rightly so.

Rat cunts, fuck off back home with fuck all to show for it.

To not have a shot on target in a Final is absolutely awful.

Tried the same old tactics, batter the opponents, try to hurt them, wind them up, foul to break up any momentum, Spain rose above it all.

Complete domination of the game from back to front from Spain, taught them a lesson.

As soon as the game switched, they started throwing themselves to the floor.

The worst losers I've ever seen in a final, a full taste of their own medicine. The aftermath is shameful and shows you exactly the mentality of that team.

Embarrassing for them, you could have played all night and you weren't scoring a goal. Football won, and rightly so.

Rat cunts, fuck off back home with fuck all to show for it.

tweets.text:
Gm ☕️

My summer is now mostly over — much grass has been touched but back at the desk and writing up a more comprehensive Outlook which will be out later today 

As mentioned last week, inflation is not what you should be focused on despite what various Fed members are touting in their speeches — impulses collapsed in May/June and that’s feeding through to below consensus prints at the same time as the narrative is talking up inflation risks (and I expect this to persist for several more months and act as headwind for yields and dollar strength here)

Liquidity conditions are about as loose as they have been from a rate of change POV and growth is okay — this is not how major market corrections and crashes start if we look at the data  

Moreover, on BTC specifically, we are now almost into August and price has continued to fail to break meaningfully below the Feb low - that is almost 6 months with $60k as support. We are only ~10 weeks away from October now — ‘the 4 year cycle bottom’ — and at what point within those 10 weeks do those awaiting that low as a buying opportunity capitulate and jump back in as the narrative pendulum creeps from ‘bear market bottom’ to ‘new bull market’? 

The ‘midterm year’ narrative has also failed spectacularly — we got the near double digit SPX correction earlier in the year and *that* was the buying opportunity for sidelineooors. I do not think you’re getting 6300 again before you get 8k+ on the index in this calendar year — that said, as publicly shared, I have been flat on levered exposure to equities since early June, pivoting some of this into BTC and fully expecting Bitcoin to outperform into 2027

Expecting more volatility *and* more upside across the board into year-end

Liquidity conditions are about as loose as they have been from a rate of change POV and growth is okay — this is not how major market corrections and crashes start if we look at the data  

Moreover, on BTC specifically, we are now almost into August and price has continued to fail to break meaningfully below the Feb low - that is almost 6 months with $60k as support. We are only ~10 weeks away from October now — ‘the 4 year cycle bottom’ — and at what point within those 10 weeks do those awaiting that low as a buying opportunity capitulate and jump back in as the narrative pendulum creeps from ‘bear market bottom’ to ‘new bull market’? 

The ‘midterm year’ narrative has also failed spectacularly — we got the near double digit SPX correction earlier in the year and *that* was the buying opportunity for sidelineooors. I do not think you’re getting 6300 again before you get 8k+ on the index in this calendar year — that said, as publicly shared, I have been flat on levered exposure to equities since early June, pivoting some of this into BTC and fully expecting Bitcoin to outperform into 2027

tweets.text:
Some updates on bottleneck timelines: 

- ABF substrates: 1 year 
/ eg. Ajinomoto (2802) for ABF film, Ibiden, Unimicron, Nan Ya PCB, etc make the substrates 

- HDI Boards: Over 6 months 
/ Victory Giant (300476), Zhen Ding (4958), Unimicron, Compeq (2313), Meiko (6787)

- Multilayer boards: ~6 months 
/ Victory Giant, WUS (002463), $TTMI, Gold Circuit Electronics (2368), 

- CCL: Over 6 months (severe shortages of materials) 
/ Elite Material (2383), Shengyi (600183), TUC (6274), ITEQ (6213), Nan Ya Plastics (1303), Resonac (4004)
Mitsui Kinzoku (5706) / HVLP copper foil.  Nittobo / glass fiber/cloth

- MLCCs, chip resistors, tantalum capacitors, aluminum electrolytic capacitors: 15-20 weeks to 1 year+

MLCC: Murata (6981), Samsung Electro-Mechanics, TDK (6762), Taiyo Yuden (6976), Yageo (2327), Walsin (2492)
Chip Resistors: Yageo, Walsin, KOA (6999), Rohm (6963) and $VSH
Tantalum capacitors: Kyocera/AVX, Yageo/KEMET, $VSH again

Aluminum electrolytic capacitors: Nippon Chemi-Con (6997) Nichicon (6996) | Timelines sourced from Digitimes, did the honors of adding in related companies. 

But it's slightly more nuanced since MLCCs and others mix. in commodity types vs. AI DCs.

Regardless, just some helpful mapping.
tweets.full_text:
Some updates on bottleneck timelines: 

- ABF substrates: 1 year 
/ eg. Ajinomoto (2802) for ABF film, Ibiden, Unimicron, Nan Ya PCB, etc make the substrates 

- HDI Boards: Over 6 months 
/ Victory Giant (300476), Zhen Ding (4958), Unimicron, Compeq (2313), Meiko (6787)

- Multilayer boards: ~6 months 
/ Victory Giant, WUS (002463), $TTMI, Gold Circuit Electronics (2368), 

- CCL: Over 6 months (severe shortages of materials) 
/ Elite Material (2383), Shengyi (600183), TUC (6274), ITEQ (6213), Nan Ya Plastics (1303), Resonac (4004)
Mitsui Kinzoku (5706) / HVLP copper foil.  Nittobo / glass fiber/cloth

- MLCCs, chip resistors, tantalum capacitors, aluminum electrolytic capacitors: 15-20 weeks to 1 year+

MLCC: Murata (6981), Samsung Electro-Mechanics, TDK (6762), Taiyo Yuden (6976), Yageo (2327), Walsin (2492)
Chip Resistors: Yageo, Walsin, KOA (6999), Rohm (6963) and $VSH
Tantalum capacitors: Kyocera/AVX, Yageo/KEMET, $VSH again

Aluminum electrolytic capacitors: Nippon Chemi-Con (6997) Nichicon (6996) | Timelines sourced from Digitimes, did the honors of adding in related companies. 

But it's slightly more nuanced since MLCCs and others mix. in commodity types vs. AI DCs.

Regardless, just some helpful mapping.

tweets.text:
Interesting point. The first one feels temporary.
The second one more durable. But hash power or energy going to Bitcoin mining doesn't determine its price (the network difficulty adjusts if miners go offline to keep the same pace of block mining). Long term, Bitcoin price is mostly a measure of how much people fear inflation, and there seems to no end in sight to democracies everywhere running deficits.
tweets.full_text:

tweets.text:
after squatting and deadlifting for a minute

it made me realize that most people in their daily non-lifting lives have no idea how to sit down, bend over to pick something up off of the floor, or squat down on the floor

in a way which minimizes the risk of injury

no one ever taught them how to do it

the result is deteriorating mobility as you get older

tweets.full_text:

2) There are tons of fundraising gimmicks like this today. Another is lying to a reporter about a fake high valuation round you're turning down to create FOMO for a real one.
